work successfully in a group environment

assessments.due_date:
Week 3 - Week 13

Presentations will be held during class
assessments.task_description:
Presentation and Discussant Task

This assessment consists of two components:

Paper Presentation:

Each student presents one academic paper from the reading list, providing a concise overview and a critical evaluation of its research design, empirical strategy, and approach to causal identification.

Discussant Role (2 occasions):

Each student will also act as discussant for two papers presented by peers.

Presentation Requirements

·      Explain the identification strategy (e.g., DiD, RD, IV, event studies) and how it is implemented.

·      Discuss the appropriateness of the design and the credibility of its assumptions.

·      Highlight strengths, limitations, and implications for causal interpretation.

·      Reading from notes will be penalised.

Lead Discussant Requirements

·      Prepare two method‑focused questions for each assigned paper.

·      Questions must address the research design, identification assumptions, empirical strategy, or robustness.

·      General or superficial questions that indicate lack of engagement will receive lower marks.

Weighting

Presentation: 20 marks

Discussant: 5 marks total

Presentations and discussions will take place throughout the semester.

AI Statement:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The focus of the presentation should be on the methods employed and how they relate to the results presented.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submissions are not accepted for in-class presentations. Failure to present at the scheduled time will result in a mark of zero.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
For this assessment, you will need to form and register a group on Blackboard

The task involves preparing a structured Literature Review and Research Proposal on a topic selected by the group from the list of topics provided by the course coordinator. After conducting a preliminary review of the relevant literature, the group must first formulate a clear research question that aligns with the selected topic area.

The literature review should focus primarily on peer‑reviewed academic research, synthesising and critically evaluating the key studies relevant to the chosen topic. The review should map the development of the research area, identify major contributions, and highlight current debates, tensions, or unresolved questions within the existing literature. The emphasis should be on understanding how prior research has approached the topic, including the theoretical foundations, empirical strategies, and main findings.

While the assessment must be grounded in academic scholarship, a limited number of high‑quality non‑academic sources may be used where they add valuable context or industry insight. Appropriate sources include reports from reputable investment banks, management consulting firms, international organisations, and established non‑profit institutions. The use of such sources should remain selective, and the academic literature must serve as the primary foundation of the review.

The literature review should not simply summarise existing work but should critically examine conceptual gaps, methodological limitations, and areas where empirical evidence remains incomplete or contested. Building on this analysis, the assessment should conclude with a concise research proposal outlining a potential empirical study motivated by the group’s synthesis of the literature. The proposal should define a clear research question, articulate its academic and practical relevance, and propose a suitable empirical strategy. This includes identifying potential data sources and specifying an appropriate research design and identification approach capable of supporting credible causal inference.

AI Statement:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This in‑person assessment requires the application of empirical research skills to real-world data. Students will analyse relationships between key variables using descriptive statistics and appropriate econometric techniques. The task assesses the ability to implement regression‑based methods, interpret empirical results, and evaluate the credibility of the evidence produced.

In line with the course’s focus, students will be required to apply causal research techniques discussed throughout the semester. This may include the use of Difference‑in‑Differences (DiD), Regression Discontinuity (RD), Instrumental Variables (IV), event-study designs, or other identification strategies. Emphasis is placed on implementing empirical methods within a clear research design and interpreting the resulting evidence in a rigorous and coherent manner.

Further details will be provided on Blackboard and discussed in class.

AI Statement:

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
